content.plain_english: This week in Year 5 maths, your child is working on Perimeter and area. The learning focuses on: use all four operations to solve problems involving measure, for example length, mass, volume and money, using decimal notation including scaling; measure and calculate the perimeter of composite rectilinear shapes in centimetres and metres; and calculate and compare the area of squares and rectangles. These skills build directly on what they have already mastered and prepare them for the next steps in the programme of study.
content.in_school: Children use practical measuring equipment — rulers, scales, measuring cylinders, and clocks — to explore measurement in real contexts. They record findings using diagrams and number lines before progressing to written calculations. Problem-solving tasks show how these skills are used every day.
content.home_activity: Use a piece of string to measure things around the house — is the sofa longer or shorter than the table? Estimate in centimetres first, then measure to check. For perimeter, measure all four sides of a book or tile and add them up.
dinner_table_questions: Can you show me how to use all four operations to solve problems involving measure, for example length, mass, volume and money, using decimal notation including scaling?
Was it easy or tricky to measure and calculate the perimeter of composite rectilinear shapes in centimetres and metres? Why?
How would you explain today's maths to someone who has never seen it before?

key_vocabulary.definition: The amount of space inside a 2-D shape, measured in square units.
A unit for measuring area — the space of a 1 cm × 1 cm square.
How long a side of the shape is.
The total distance around the outside edge of a shape.
How long something is from one end to the other.

example_questions: Find the area of a triangle with base 8 cm and height 5 cm.
A cuboid is 4 cm × 3 cm × 5 cm. Find its volume.
Find the perimeter of the compound shape.
content.times_table_tip: By Year 5 children should know all their times tables with speed and confidence. Ask quick-fire questions at dinner — mixing tables so they recall facts rather than just reciting sequences. Can they answer in under 3 seconds?
